summary: New managers often inadvertently stifle the career growth of individual contributors by holding onto technical work and decision-making authority they should be delegating. The post identifies five common pitfalls: doing all technical design work, hoarding project management, neglecting non-technical feedback, keeping information to themselves, and focusing on personal output over team development. Fournier argues that managers must shift from additive value (their own contributions) to multiplicative value (amplifying their team's output). The key insight is that new managers need to actively create space for ICs to grow by stepping back from the technical leadership role they recently held, which benefits both the IC's career trajectory and the manager's ability to scale.
key_points: New managers must deliberately step back from technical design work to create growth opportunities for ICs, even when those ICs lack equivalent skills
Teaching ICs project management is painful but essential for their career advancement and for the manager's ability to scale
Managers who only give technical feedback reinforce the perception that management is the only path to authority
Information hoarding turns ICs into 'doers' while managers become 'deciders,' undermining IC ownership and engagement
A manager's output is measured by team results, not personal contributions—the goal is multiplicative rather than additive value
money_quotes: If you respond by filling in for their skill gaps, you are going to quickly hit two problems. First, you won't be able to scale because you'll be too busy doing technical stuff to take on a bigger team. Second, you won't be able to scale because you won't have a person to whom you can delegate.
Teaching someone how to run a project is painful, and they will often say that they don't want to do the work, don't want to learn it, and make your life difficult in the process.
The result is the impression that management is the way to have technical authority over a group, which leads ICs to wonder what the technical track is even for.
When you don't give your team the context for the work and just pass on tasks and work items to them, you make it clear that they are simply 'doers' and your job is the job of 'decider.'
If it's your code that gets you over the finish line for every project, you aren't providing multiplicative value for the team, you're providing the additive value of your work as an engineer.
New managers, make sure that you aren't trying to be a senior engineer who has direct reports.
key_insight: New managers must transition from being high-performing individual contributors to creating leverage through team development, which means deliberately stepping back from technical work to make room for others to grow.
